column2: Deepfake & AI Impersonation Law Tracker (as of 2026-07-22)

column13: scope_or_obligations
column14: penalties
column16: private_right_of_action

column9: Signed 2025-04-28. Platform compliance deadline passed 2026-05-19. FTC stood up TakeItDown.ftc.gov as a reporting portal. No public FTC enforcement cases under the Act as of 2026-07-10.
column12: Signed into law: 2025-04-28; Platform compliance deadline (365 days from enactment): 2026-05-19
column13: Covered platforms must remove intimate images, including AI-generated synthetic intimate images, within 48 hours of a valid report. Platforms had until 2026-05-19 to stand up removal processes.
column14: FTC enforces under FTC Act Section 5. Civil penalties per 16 CFR 1.98 ($53,088 per violation as of the 2024 adjustment). Criminal penalties for knowing distribution of non-consensual intimate images, including deepfake images, with up to two years imprisonment.

column4: Government and Business Impersonation Rule (16 CFR Part 461)
column9: Government and business impersonation provisions effective 2024-04-01. The proposed extension to individual impersonation (SNPRM) is not finalized as of 2026-07-10.
column13: Makes impersonation of government entities and businesses, including via AI-generated content, an unfair or deceptive act under the FTC Act. Enables the FTC to seek civil penalties and consumer redress.
column14: Civil penalties under FTC Act Section 5(m)(1)(A); consumer redress under Section 19. The individual-impersonation extension was proposed as an SNPRM but has not been finalized.

column4: FCC Declaratory Ruling: AI-Generated Voices Under TCPA
column9: Declaratory ruling released 2024-02-08. Immediately effective; it clarifies existing TCPA authority rather than creating new rules.
column13: Clarifies that AI-generated voices are "artificial" voices under 47 U.S.C. 227(b)(1), making AI-voice robocalls to residential lines without prior express consent violations of the TCPA.
column14: Existing TCPA penalties apply: up to $500 per violation, trebled to $1,500 for willful or knowing violations. State attorneys general may also enforce.

column4: FCC NPRM: AI-Generated Calls and Texts
column9: NPRM released 2024-08-08. Comment period closed. No final rule issued as of 2026-07-10.
column13: Proposes additional rules for AI-generated calls and texts, including disclosure requirements and consent frameworks. Not finalized.
column14: Not applicable (proposed rule, not in force).

column4: FCC Consent Decree: Lingo Telecom (AI Deepfake Robocalls)
column9: Consent decree entered 2024-08-21 for STIR/SHAKEN attestation violations tied to transmission of AI deepfake robocalls impersonating President Biden.
column13: Requires Lingo Telecom to pay $1,000,000 civil penalty, implement compliance plan, and adopt Know Your Customer / Know Your Upstream Provider controls for STIR/SHAKEN attestation.
column14: $1,000,000 civil penalty.

column4: FCC Forfeiture Order: Steve Kramer (AI Deepfake Robocalls)
column9: Forfeiture order adopted 2024-09-26, released 2024-09-30. FCC referred Kramer to DOJ for collection 2024-12-03.
column12: Notice of apparent liability issued: 2024-05-24; Forfeiture order adopted: 2024-09-26; Referred to DOJ for collection: 2024-12-03
column13: $6,000,000 forfeiture for illegal robocalls carrying deepfake AI-generated voice messages impersonating President Biden, in violation of the Truth in Caller ID Act.
column14: $6,000,000 forfeiture.

column9: Passed the Senate 2026-01-13. Not passed by the House. Not enacted as of 2026-07-10.
column13: Would create a federal civil cause of action for victims of non-consensual deepfake intimate images, with statutory damages and injunctive relief. Not enacted.
column14: Not applicable (bill, not law). Would provide statutory damages if enacted.

column9: Introduced in the 118th Congress; reintroduced in the 119th. Not enacted as of 2026-07-10.
column13: Would create a federal right of publicity for AI-generated digital replicas of voice and likeness. Not enacted.
column14: Not applicable (bill, not law). Would provide statutory damages and injunctive relief if enacted.

column13: Prohibits distribution of deceptive deepfake audio or video of candidates within 60 days of an election with intent to injure the candidate's reputation or deceive a voter.
column14: No criminal penalty. Injunctive relief; civil liability.
column16: Yes, candidates may seek injunctive relief.

column13: Signed into law 2024-09-17. Extends the deepfake election prohibition to 120 days before an election, adds platform takedown duties for deceptive AI-generated election content, and expands injunctive and damages remedies. A federal court has temporarily blocked some of these provisions on First Amendment grounds; which parts are currently enforceable depends on the latest court orders.
column14: Injunctive relief; civil damages. Platforms face mandatory content labeling and removal obligations (subject to court orders).
column16: Yes.
column17: A federal court (E.D. Cal.) issued a preliminary injunction blocking portions of AB 2839 in Kohls v. Bonta (2:24-cv-02527). X Corp v. Bonta raised similar First Amendment challenges. Which provisions remain enforceable depends on the most recent court orders; check the docket for current status.

column13: Enacted text would require large online platforms to label or remove deceptive AI-generated election content. Companion bill to AB 2839. Like AB 2839, portions may be affected by ongoing federal court litigation; current enforceability depends on the latest court orders.
column14: Civil penalties for platform non-compliance (subject to court orders).
column16: Platform-directed duties; individual claims are under AB 2839.
column17: May be affected by the same federal court litigation challenging AB 2839. Which platform duties are currently enforceable depends on the most recent court orders; check the AB 2839 docket for status.

column13: First state deepfake election law. Prohibits creating or distributing a deepfake video intended to injure a candidate or influence an election within 30 days of an election.
column14: Class A misdemeanor.
column16: Not an express private right; criminal enforcement.

column13: Criminal prohibition on using deepfake technology to influence elections. Prohibits disseminating deepfake media depicting a candidate within 90 days of an election without disclosure.
column14: Tiered criminal penalties: up to 90 days or $1,000 in the base case; up to 364 days or $3,000 where there is intent to cause violence or bodily harm; up to five years or $10,000 for a repeat offense within five years. A convicted candidate forfeits the nomination or office.
column16: The depicted individual, an injured candidate, the attorney general, or a county or city attorney may seek injunctive relief; the offense itself is criminal.
column17: Challenged on First Amendment grounds. Current litigation status not independently confirmed as of 2026-07-10.

column13: Prohibits distribution of synthetic media depicting a candidate in a false manner in an election campaign without a disclosure label.
column14: Injunctive relief; civil penalties. Candidates may seek damages.
column16: Yes, candidates may seek injunctive relief and damages.

column13: Requires AI-generated content in political advertisements and electioneering communications to include a specified disclaimer.
column14: Criminal and civil penalties for missing disclaimers, with a complaint process and expedited hearings.
column16: A person may file a complaint under the act's expedited procedures; candidates depicted may seek relief.

column13: Creates a cause of action against the sponsor of an electioneering communication that uses synthetic media to alter a candidate's appearance, action, or speech without a clear and conspicuous disclosure. The act also establishes AI governance requirements for Kentucky state government; the election provisions amend KRS 117.001 and add KRS 117.322.
column14: No standalone criminal offense for the synthetic media itself. Failure to comply with a court-ordered disclosure is subject to the campaign finance penalties in KRS 121.990(3). Media outlets and their advertising representatives are generally not liable, with limited exceptions.
column16: Yes; an affected candidate may seek injunctive or other equitable relief requiring the disclosure, with attorney fees and costs available to the prevailing party, on clear and convincing evidence.

column13: Requires a clear and understandable disclosure on electioneering communications that use artificial intelligence to create, alter, or digitally manipulate the image, likeness, or voice of a candidate or of an elected official subject to recall so that it falsely appears to a reasonable observer to be an authentic record of actual speech or conduct. Signed June 9, 2026; the tracker previously miscited the unrelated 2025 HB 459.
column14: Criminal: violating R.S. 18:1463 with intent to injure the reputation of a candidate or elected official subject to recall, or to otherwise deceive a voter, carries a fine of up to $2,000, imprisonment for up to two years with or without hard labor, or both. Media entities that merely broadcast paid content over which they have no editorial control are exempt.
column16: No express private right of action in the AI disclosure provisions; enforcement is criminal.

column13: Requires campaign advertising that contains synthetic media of a candidate, manipulated or AI-generated content likely to deceive a reasonable person about the candidate's appearance, speech, or conduct, to carry the disclosure 'THIS COMMUNICATION CONTAINS AUDIO, VIDEO AND/OR IMAGES THAT HAVE BEEN MANIPULATED OR ALTERED', with satire and parody excluded. Carried over from the 2025 session and signed March 23, 2026.
column14: Civil penalties assessed by the Commission on Governmental Ethics and Election Practices: up to 500% of the expenditure amount for a synthetic-media disclosure violation, versus the 100% cap for other Section 1014 violations. The Attorney General may seek injunctive relief and recover costs and fees.
column16: No new private right of action; the law does not limit other causes of action a person may have.

column13: Prohibits knowingly, or with reckless disregard, creating, using, or disseminating a deepfake to produce materially false information with intent to influence voting, misrepresent election facts, or induce petition signatures, with satire, parody, broadcaster, and Section 230 exemptions. Also directs the State Administrator of Elections to correct credible reports of election misinformation and authorizes injunctions and subpoenas in limited cases. Approved May 12, 2026; the tracker previously miscited the withdrawn 2025 SB 141.
column14: Misdemeanor: a fine of up to $5,000, imprisonment for up to five years, or both. The State Board of Elections may also bring a civil action to recover correction costs, damages, court costs, and attorney fees on clear and convincing evidence.
column16: No general private right of action; enforcement runs through the State Administrator, the State Board, and criminal prosecution.

column13: General digital-forgery offense, not an election-specific statute: criminalizes creating and distributing as genuine a forged digital likeness, a computer-generated visual or audio representation of an identifiable individual that materially misrepresents their appearance, speech, or behavior and is likely to deceive a reasonable person, made without consent and with intent to defraud or injure. Deceptive political deepfakes can fall within it only when those general elements are met.
column14: First-degree misdemeanor generally; third-degree felony when committed through a scheme to defraud, coerce, or commit theft. Affirmative defense where the defendant took reasonable action to put viewers or listeners on notice that the content is not genuine; exceptions for constitutionally protected activity, law enforcement duties, and technology providers.
column16: No; criminal enforcement only.

column13: Criminalizes distributing materially deceptive media with intent to influence an upcoming election, subject to statutory exceptions, and permits permanent injunctive relief.
column16: Yes, limited to permanent injunctive relief for statutorily authorized plaintiffs.

column13: During the 90 days before an election, a creator may not knowingly create and distribute a deceptive and fraudulent deepfake of a candidate on the ballot unless the media conspicuously discloses that it contains AI-generated content.
column14: Civil penalty under A.R.S. 16-937(B) for each day the undisclosed deepfake is distributed.
column16: No express private cause of action.

column13: Prohibits knowingly or recklessly distributing an undisclosed or improperly disclosed deepfake communication about a candidate during the 60 days before a primary or 90 days before a general election.
column14: Administrative civil penalty of at least $100 per violation for unpaid advertising, or at least 10% of the amount paid for paid advertising.
column16: Yes; a depicted candidate may seek an injunction, damages including punitive damages, fees, and costs.

column13: From the first working day of February in even-numbered years through the general election, prohibits reckless distribution of materially deceptive media that risks harming a candidate or changing voting behavior, subject to exceptions.
column16: Yes; a depicted individual or qualifying voter organization may obtain damages and equitable relief, and violating a court order can draw a civil fine of up to $1,000 per day.

column13: Election deepfake regulation.
Requires a prescribed disclaimer on campaign communications containing fabricated media that depicts a candidate, including materially altered recordings and lifelike AI imitations.
column14: No standalone criminal fine; a depicted candidate may seek actual damages, injunctive relief, attorney fees, and costs against responsible sponsors or payors.
column16: Yes.

column13: Prohibits knowingly disseminating a nonconsensual digitization within 90 days of an election when intended to injure a candidate, influence an election result, or deter voting.
column16: Yes, for statutorily authorized injunctive relief.

column13: Requires disclaimers on AI-generated campaign advertisements and prohibits knowing distribution of materially deceptive election media under the Campaign Reporting Act.
column16: Yes, at least for statutory injunctive relief.

column13: Requires disclosure when a political communication contains materially deceptive media and the distributor or publisher has actual knowledge of the deception, with satire, news, broadcaster, platform, and federal-law exceptions.
column14: No standalone monetary fine in subdivision 5; enforcement is principally expedited injunctive relief with recoverable court costs and attorney fees.
column16: Yes; a candidate whose voice or likeness appears in the media may bring the expedited action.

column13: Requires campaign communications containing synthetic media to disclose that the image, audio, or video has been manipulated, with platform, news, broadcaster, periodical, satire, and parody exceptions.
column14: Court-imposed civil penalty of up to $10,000 per violation; the statutory enforcement remedy is exclusive.
column16: No general private right; enforcement is assigned to the Secretary of State or Attorney General.

column13: Requires audible, visual, and digital-provenance disclosures for paid audio or visual communications intended to influence voting on a candidate or ballot proposition when the communication contains synthetic media. The disclosure duties took effect May 1, 2024; the sentencing aggravator took effect July 1, 2024.
column14: Civil penalty of up to $1,000 per violation; knowing use of AI to facilitate a crime is a sentencing aggravator under 76-3-203.18, effective July 1, 2024.
column16: Yes; the statute permits a civil action to enforce the disclosure section.

column13: Requires a prescribed disclosure when a political communication sponsored by a covered person or committee contains synthetic media.

Requires disclosures for qualifying political advertisements created in whole or substantial part using generative AI, as part of Michigan's 2023 multi-bill election-deepfake package (HBs 5141 to 5145).

Treats AI-generated digital replicas and deceptive government-style social-media content as election deception or forgery when reasonably likely to prevent or dissuade registering, voting, or supporting a candidate or public question; not a general political-ad disclosure law.
column14: Conduct satisfying 10 ILCS 5/29-4 is a Class 4 felony.
column16: Only through the existing 10 ILCS 5/29-18 conspiracy liability framework, not a standalone deepfake cause of action.

column13: General criminal and civil liability for creating or disseminating deceptive audio or visual media for specified unlawful purposes. The statute is broader than elections and is not a dedicated pre-election deepfake rule, but it reaches deceptive election media used for covered unlawful purposes.
column16: Yes, under the civil-remedy section, 2C:21-17.8.

column13: During the 90 days before an election, bars distributing an AI-generated deepfake of a candidate, election official, or party on a state or local ballot when the distributor knew or should have known it was a deepfake, unless the required disclosure is included.
column14: Primarily equitable and civil relief rather than a standalone administrative fine.
column16: Yes; a depicted candidate or election official may seek injunctive or equitable relief and damages against the sponsor, with fees and costs available.

column13: Criminalizes creation and distribution of AI-generated non-consensual intimate images (synthetic sexual content).
column14: State jail felony (up to two years, $10,000 fine).

column13: Civil remedies for victims of non-consensual intimate images, including AI-generated intimate images.
column14: Civil liability; damages and injunctive relief.
column16: Yes.

column13: Criminalizes knowingly or purposely disclosing explicit synthetic media, deepfake intimate imagery of an identifiable individual created or altered with AI, without consent, including threats to disclose for money or other consideration.
column14: First offense: fine up to $1,000, up to one year in county jail, or both. Second or subsequent offense: fine up to $10,000, up to five years in state prison, or both; up to ten years where the depicted person is under 18.

column13: Civil liability for distribution of sexually explicit deepfake images or recordings created without consent.
column14: Civil damages, including economic and non-economic damages, punitive damages, and attorney fees.
column16: Yes.

column4: ELVIS Act, Tenn. Code Ann. 47-25-1701 et seq.
column5: ELVIS Act, Tenn. Code Ann. 47-25-1701 et seq.
column13: Extends Tennessee's right of publicity to cover AI-generated replicas of voice and likeness. Protects against unauthorized use of a person's voice or visual likeness through AI or other technology.
column14: Civil liability: actual damages or profits, injunctive relief, attorney fees. Statutory damages available.
column16: Yes.

column13: Protects deceased performers' digital replicas. Extends the right of publicity to cover unauthorized use of a deceased personality's digital replica, including AI-generated likenesses.
column14: Civil liability: damages, profits, injunctive relief.
column16: Yes, representatives of deceased personalities.

column13: Requires informed consent before using AI to create digital replicas of performers in contracts. Makes contract provisions for AI replicas unenforceable if the performer was not represented or did not have a meaningful opportunity to consult representation.
column14: Contract remedy: unenforceable provisions. Civil liability for unauthorized use.
column16: Yes.

column4: N.Y. Civil Rights Law 50-f (S.5959-D/A.5605-C, L. 2020 ch. 304)
column5: N.Y. Civil Rights Law 50-f (S.5959-D/A.5605-C, L. 2020 ch. 304)
column13: Regulates unauthorized commercial and certain expressive uses of a deceased performer's or deceased personality's highly realistic digital replica.
column14: Civil damages and related remedies.
column16: Yes.

column13: Renders insufficiently specific contractual grants for replacing a living individual's performance with a digital replica void and unenforceable.
column14: Contractual unenforceability rather than a criminal penalty.
column16: Yes, through ordinary contract litigation or defense.
